lede: Twenty weeks, build-first. From Python to a deployed, JVM-native capstone, then deep into evaluation, retrieval, fine-tuning math, inference economics, AI system design, and the portfolio polish that converts builds into offers. Every day: a focus, linked resources, and a hands-on task.

courses.name: Ed Donner — AI Engineer Core Track
courses.description: 8-wk backbone · RAG, QLoRA, agents, LiteLLM, prompt caching

courses.name: AI Engineer Agentic Track — Agent & MCP Course
courses.description: 6-wk · 8 projects · OpenAI SDK, CrewAI, LangGraph, AutoGen, MCP

courses.name: AI Engineer Production Track — Deploy at Scale
courses.description: DevOps layer · AWS Bedrock/SageMaker, Lambda, Terraform, CI/CD

courses.name: AI Coder — Vibe Coder → Agentic Engineer
courses.description: Spine for W1 · Claude Code deep, Agent Teams, Agent SDK, autonomous Jira→PR

resourceMap.name: Ed Donner — AI Coder
resourceMap.bestFor: Claude Code mastery, agentic coding, Agent Teams, Agent SDK

resourceMap.name: Ed Donner — Core Track
resourceMap.bestFor: LLM APIs, RAG, QLoRA, agents from scratch

resourceMap.name: Ed Donner — Agentic Track
resourceMap.bestFor: OpenAI SDK, CrewAI, LangGraph, AutoGen, MCP

resourceMap.name: Ed Donner — Production Track
resourceMap.bestFor: AWS, Docker, Terraform, CI/CD, SaaS, MLOps

resourceMap.name: ByteByteGo — Become an AI Engineer
resourceMap.bestFor: Pre-training, reasoning models, agent reasoning, multimodal

resourceMap.name: Java Brains — From Java to AI
resourceMap.bestFor: Code-free LLM on-ramp + JVM integration framing (Spring AI / LangChain4j)

resourceMap.name: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

paragraphs: Budget ~10 hrs/week: ~2 hrs read/watch, ~6 hrs coding, ~2 hrs writing (a README or short post — this is where it snaps into your head). The single biggest predictor of retention is the build-to-theory ratio.
The pattern that works for engineers like you:

Two cadences: the build phase (Weeks 0–10) is hands-keyboard, capstone-driven — don't stall on theory. The depth phase (Weeks 11–18) is what makes you senior in AI, not just functional: it's anchored on Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (O'Reilly) — keep the book open as the spine for W11–16, then AI system design (W12B) and interview prep (W18) close it out.
Don't fall for: tutorial hell, framework-collecting, chasing every model release. Pick one frontier API (Anthropic) + one open-source path (HuggingFace + Ollama) and stick to them.
bullets: Read the topic intro (15 min) — skim, don't deep-read.
Build the smallest thing that demonstrates the concept, with no framework. Highest-leverage step.
Rebuild with the framework — now you see what it was hiding.
Write a 200-word note: "what I'd use this for at work."

items: Editor: VS Code + Python/Jupyter extensions, or PyCharm if you want JetBrains-familiar.
API keys: Anthropic ($5 free), OpenAI ($5), Groq (free tier, fast inference for experiments).
Local models: install Ollama, pull llama3.2:3b and qwen2.5:7b.
Vector DB: start with Chroma (in-process, zero-ops). Move to pgvector/Qdrant later.
Observability: sign up for Langfuse (open-source, self-hostable).
Git: one repo ai-learning-lab, one folder per week.
Claude Code: install Claude Code now — Week 1 turns it into your daily driver.

parts.title: Foundations & Build
parts.sub: Weeks 0–10 · to a deployed capstone · 12 weeks

weeks.title: Python Pre-Prep & Environment
weeks.goal: Goal: stop fighting Python. Be fluent enough that the language is invisible by Week 1. No AI yet — pure language + tooling delta from Kotlin.

days.focusSub: Syntax map, type hints, the traps

resources.label: Real Python — Python for Java devs

days.hands: Rewrite a Kotlin CSV transformer in Python with full type hints. Exercism Python: 3 starter exercises.
Exercism: 4–5 exercises hitting comprehensions, dicts, generators.

resources.label: Real Python — Async IO in Python

days.hands: Async httpx fetcher hitting 5 URLs concurrently; time it vs sequential.

days.focusSub: Your structured-output validator all course long
days.hands: Model a billpay Payment as a Pydantic model with validators + a failing-validation test.

days.focusSub: Your Quarkus comfort zone, in Python
days.hands: FastAPI service: 2 endpoints (1 async) + 1 pytest. Commit to ai-learning-lab/week-00.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none — courses assume Python. Don't pay for syntax.
weeks.deliverable: Async FastAPI service + Pydantic models, typed, tested.

weeks.title: Claude Code & Agentic Coding
weeks.goal: Goal — front-loaded on purpose. You already use Claude Code daily; this week turns it into an autonomous coding system you drive efficiently, then pushes into multi-agent coding. Spine: Ed Donner — AI Coder. You'll use these skills to build every later project, so the payoff compounds.

days.hands: Write a real CLAUDE.md for your Billpay repo: build/lint/test commands, module map, conventions (Exposed, Temporal, KSP). Run one real task end-to-end.
Create 2 skills in .claude/skills/: one to generate Docusaurus pages from KSP variant metadata, one /pr-summary. Invoke both.

days.focusSub: Run 100% of the time, unlike CLAUDE.md
days.hands: Add a PreToolUse hook that blocks force-push to main + scans writes for secrets (exit 2 = deny), and a PostToolUse hook running ktlint/detekt after edits. Verify they fire every time.

resources.label: AI Coder — Wk3 (Agent Teams, Ralph Loops)

days.hands: Run 3 subagents concurrently to review auth, payments, migrations. Use plan mode for a multi-file refactor; try a Ralph Loop (persistent self-correcting agent) on a sandboxed task; roll back via checkpoint.
Run claude -p headless in a GitHub Action / pre-commit. Wire an autonomous loop: raise a Jira issue → agent builds, tests, opens a PR. Connect a GitHub MCP server; skim the Agent SDK + Cowork.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: AI Coder (spine) + official docs.
weeks.deliverable: Billpay repo: CLAUDE.md + 2 skills + 2 hooks + CI PR-review + autonomous Jira→PR loop demo.

weeks.title: Gen AI Building Blocks
weeks.goal: Goal: understand how LLMs actually work, and ship 3 LLM-powered scripts from raw API calls — no framework.

days.hands: Note the $/M-token cost of 3 models you'll use. Sketch when each fits.
Tokenize 3 sample inputs; predict then verify token counts.
Work notebooks ch.1–6. Build 1: CLI that summarizes a dir of markdown notes (raw anthropic SDK).
Build 2: extractor — job description → {role, seniority, skills[], remote} as a Pydantic model.
Build 3: LLM that can call 3 tools — get_weather, search_files, run_sql.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 1
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-02-basics + README on tokens, context windows, cost math.

weeks.title: JVM-Native AI — Spring AI & LangChain4j
weeks.goal: Goal — your unfair advantage. Re-implement what you just learned in Python (LLM APIs, tool use, structured output) in your real stack — LangChain4j / Spring AI on Quarkus — so you can ship AI into Billpay without leaving the JVM. Almost no AI-engineering learner can do this; you already own the stack. On-ramp: Java Brains — From Java to AI (skim if any concept feels shaky).

days.focusSub: Spring AI vs LangChain4j vs Quarkus-LangChain4j
days.hands: Add quarkus-langchain4j to a Quarkus project; wire an Anthropic/OpenAI chat model; get your first completion from Kotlin.
Port your W2 summarizer to a LangChain4j AiServices interface; add a system prompt + temperature; stream the response.

days.focusSub: map LLM output → Kotlin data classes
days.hands: Rebuild your W2 extractor to return a typed Kotlin data class directly — no manual JSON parsing.

days.focusTitle: Tool calling & RAG on the JVM
days.hands: Expose 2–3 @Tool methods (incl. a read-only Oracle query); build a minimal RAG over Temporal docs using a pgvector embedding store.
Add chat memory + robust error handling (retry on malformed output, typed fallback) to your service. This is the pattern your capstone will ship on.

weeks.udemy: Resources: Java Brains (on-ramp) + LangChain4j / Spring AI / Quarkus docs
weeks.deliverable: Repo billpay-ai-jvm — Quarkus/Kotlin service: tool-using, RAG-backed, typed, memory-aware LLM endpoint.

weeks.title: Context Engineering & RAG
weeks.goal: Goal: build RAG from scratch (no framework), then rebuild with a framework. Corpus: Temporal docs — eat your own dog food. (Deep retrieval theory comes back in Week 13.)

days.hands: Write a 1-pager: 3 chunking strategies + when each wins.
Ingest Temporal docs; chunk 3 ways; embed with all-MiniLM-L6-v2.
Add a re-ranker (cohere/rerank free or bge-reranker-v2-m3). Compare with/without.
Rebuild in LlamaIndex/LangChain; implement hybrid + HyDE + query rewriting.

days.focusSub: pgvector swap · HNSW vs IVF · when to pick Qdrant/Weaviate/Pinecone

resources.label: Hamel — most RAG problems are retrieval problems
Qdrant benchmarks (vs pgvector / Weaviate / Pinecone / Milvus)

days.hands: Swap Chroma → pgvector; record recall@5 / MRR across chunking strategies in results.md. Then write a one-pager: HNSW vs IVF (when each wins on recall / latency / memory) + a decision matrix — Chroma / pgvector / Qdrant / Weaviate / Pinecone — scored on millions-of-vectors, metadata filtering, multi-tenancy, self-host vs SaaS.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 5
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-03-rag — answers "what is sticky queue starvation?" with citations + numbers + vector-DB decision matrix.

weeks.title: Agents — Build Without Frameworks
weeks.goal: Goal: write a ReAct loop in ~100 lines. Once you grok this, every framework becomes a thin wrapper in your mind.

days.hands: Diagram the ReAct loop on paper before writing any code.

days.focusSub: The most important exercise in the course
days.hands: Write the loop yourself with 5 tools: web search (Tavily free), file r/w, python REPL, calculator, current_date.
Sketch each Anthropic pattern as a flow.
Each pattern as a standalone example. Note: when would you use each inside a Temporal workflow?

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Agentic Track (foundations) + Core Track Wk 8
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-04-agents-from-scratch — ReAct + 5 patterns + Temporal-context comparison.

weeks.title: Multi-Framework Agents + MCP
weeks.goal: Goal: become fluent across the four frameworks the Agentic Track teaches — build the same agent in each, then form opinions on each vs a real workflow engine. Breadth here makes you fluent in any team's stack.

days.focusSub: state, nodes, edges, checkpointer, HITL ≈ Temporal
days.hands: Port your W4 ReAct agent to LangGraph; add a checkpointer + human-in-the-loop interrupt. Note where this mirrors Temporal signals.
Rebuild the same agent in the OpenAI Agents SDK; compare its handoff & guardrail primitives to LangGraph edges.
A 3-role crew (researcher → analyst → writer) producing a billpay incident summary.
A group-chat of agents solving a task; note how orchestration/turn-taking differs from CrewAI roles.

days.focusSub: build your demo server, then judge them all
days.hands: Build: an MCP server exposing your Oracle DB read-only (reused later). Write "4 frameworks vs Temporal as a workflow engine" — a portfolio piece only you can write.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Agentic Track (full course) — OpenAI SDK · CrewAI · LangGraph · AutoGen · MCP
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-05-frameworks — same agent ×4 + Oracle MCP server + comparison doc.

weeks.title: Fine-Tuning & Local Models
weeks.goal: Goal: know when fine-tuning helps (rarely) and how to do it when it does. Decision tree: prompt → few-shot → RAG → fine-tune. (The memory math & quantization theory deepen in Week 14.)

resources.label: Hamel — Fine-Tuning is Probably Not For You

days.hands: Write your escalation decision tree; mark where billpay tasks would land.
Fine-tune Qwen2.5-3B with LoRA via unsloth on a free Colab T4. Task: SQL gen on a synthetic billpay schema.
Serve your fine-tuned model with vLLM; benchmark tokens/sec vs Ollama.
Pre-train a tiny GPT on Tiny Shakespeare. Optional but you'll never see transformers the same way.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 7
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-06-finetuning — LoRA model beating base on held-out test + "when would I fine-tune at work?" writeup.

weeks.title: Evals, Observability & Production Basics
weeks.goal: Goal: the most undervalued skill in AI engineering. After this week you're in the top 10% purely because most people skip evals. (The measurement theory — perplexity, judges, ranking — goes deep in Week 12.)

days.hands: List the failure modes you expect in your Week 5 agent.
On your W5 agent: label 100 traces → cluster failures → LLM-as-judge per mode → validate (TPR/TNR) → golden set of 20–30 that gate CI.
Instrument every agent call (cost, latency, tools). Dashboard: "which tool calls fail most?"
Add I/O guardrails. Try 10 attacks; document mitigations. (Expanded in Week 16.)

weeks.udemy: Udemy: weak fit. Use the practitioners above; Maven AI Evals if you later invest.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-07-evals — eval suite + Langfuse dashboard + 3 judges w/ TPR/TNR + red-team doc.

weeks.title: MLOps & Experiment Tracking
weeks.goal: Goal: add the ML lifecycle layer that observability (W7) doesn't cover — prompt / dataset / model versioning, experiment tracking, model registry. Once this is wired, every later result you ship is reproducible and every model upgrade can be gated. This is what makes "AI Engineer" sit next to "MLOps" on a job spec.

days.focusSub: MLflow + Weights & Biases — the runs/params/metrics primitive
days.hands: Re-run your W6 LoRA fine-tune with MLflow tracking: log hyperparams, train/eval loss curves, the LoRA adapter as an artifact, the eval-set metrics. Add a second run with a different learning rate; compare runs in the UI.

resources.label: Hamel — A field guide to prompt management

days.hands: Move your capstone's 3 hottest prompts into Langfuse with versions. In code, fetch by name+label (e.g. billpay-classifier:production). Run a quick A/B between v1 and v2; record judge scores on each.

days.focusSub: MLflow Registry / HF Hub — "which model is in prod?"
days.hands: Push the W6 LoRA adapter to HF Hub with a model card (training data, eval scores, intended use, known failure modes). Register in MLflow with stages staging → production. Document the promotion criteria.

days.focusSub: DVC / HF Datasets — eval sets and training data have versions too

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Monitoring ML in Production

days.hands: Version your W7 golden eval set with DVC; tag eval-v1 after CI passes. Sketch a drift detector — embedding-distance histogram between training distribution and a week of production traces.

days.focusSub: GitHub Actions + your W7 evals as the gate

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Continuous Integration for ML

days.hands: GitHub Actions workflow on PR: run your W7 eval suite against the proposed prompt/model version, fail PR if any judge regresses below threshold. Wire MLflow run URL into the PR comment. This is the foundation W16 generalises to full deploy gating.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: weak fit — use docs above + W&B Courses (free) for depth.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-07b-mlops — MLflow runs + Langfuse prompt versions + HF Hub model + DVC-versioned eval set + eval-gated CI workflow.

weeks.title: Temporal + LLM Orchestration
weeks.goal: Goal — YOUR DIFFERENTIATOR. No course teaches this. Run agents as durable, throttled, observable Temporal workflows at 500K+ scale. This is why you're hireable; the capstone is built on it.

days.hands: Wrap each LLM call as an Activity; keep non-determinism out of Workflow code. Port your W5 agent into a Temporal workflow skeleton.
Add backoff retries + timeouts to LLM/tool Activities; make tool side-effects idempotent.
Add an approval gate via Signal; expose live agent state via Query; executeUpdateWithStart for kick-off + first input.

resources.label: Temporal Schedules · task-queue partitioning · worker tuning

days.hands: Trigger 500K workflows throttled — rate-limit LLM calls, cap concurrency, protect the r8g.8xlarge Postgres without scaling it. Schedules vs Workflow.sleep() at scale.

resources.label: Blog: "What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration"

days.hands: Draft the post — it basically writes itself with your background. Your standout interview asset.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none exists — this is uniquely yours.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-08-temporal-llm — durable multi-agent workflow w/ HITL signals + throttled fan-out + blog draft.

weeks.title: Capstone — Billpay Ops Copilot
weeks.goal: Goal: ship one real, end-to-end agentic system you'd demo to a hiring manager. Uses everything: structured outputs, RAG, multi-framework agents, MCP, evals, Langfuse, and Temporal orchestration. No tutorials — just build. Build it JVM-native — Quarkus + Quarkus-LangChain4j + the Temporal Java SDK, reusing your Week 2B work. The Python weeks were for learning; the capstone ships on your stack, which makes it a far stronger portfolio piece for your background.

days.hands: Design doc (architecture, tradeoffs); implement the happy-path diagnosis on one failure class.
Wire Temporal API tool, Oracle read MCP, runbook RAG, metrics tool.
Temporal orchestration, retries/timeouts, HITL approval signal, Langfuse tracing.
Deploy to Fly.io / Modal (full AWS deploy comes in Week 16). Smoke test end-to-end.
≥10 golden incidents in the eval suite; 1500-word writeup on architecture, tradeoffs, "what I'd do at 10× scale."
Pin repo; blog post; LinkedIn + Loom demo. Resume bullets that lead with outcomes ("cut RAG P95 800ms→180ms via hybrid + reranker"), not tech names.
Subscribe: Hamel, Eugene Yan, Latent Space. Open Chip Huyen's book — Part II starts.

weeks.udemy: Synthesis — Agentic Track Capstone + Core Track Wk 8 for reference patterns.
weeks.deliverable: Deployed Billpay Ops Copilot + eval suite + writeup + portfolio assets.

specs.heading: The build — recommended pick (strongest for your portfolio)
specs.body: An agent that diagnoses a stuck Temporal workflow given a workflow ID, then proposes (and optionally executes, behind a human-approval signal) a mitigation. It sits exactly at the intersection of your strengths and AI engineering.
bullets: Input: a workflow ID (or an alert webhook).

Tools / MCP servers: query Temporal API (history, pending activities, stuck task queues); read Oracle business state via your Week-5 read-only MCP server; RAG over runbooks + Temporal docs (Week 3); a metrics tool (shard latency, sync-match rate).

Reasoning: a Quarkus-LangChain4j agent (or LangGraph if you'd rather stay in Python) classifies the failure (sticky-queue starvation, poller starvation, IO:XactSync contention, prepared-statement pinning, …) and drafts a mitigation.
Orchestration: the whole diagnosis runs as a Temporal workflow (Java SDK) — durable, retryable, with a human-approval Signal before any write action.
Quality gates: eval suite (Week 7) with golden incidents; LLM-as-judge on diagnosis correctness.
Observability: full Langfuse tracing of every tool call and token.

specs.heading: Alternative picks (if you want a change of domain)
bullets: Code Review Agent — reviews Kotlin PRs against your team style guide + anti-patterns; RAG over the guide + history.
India Relocation Concierge — multi-step planning (schools, neighborhoods, doc checklists). Personally useful, less technically flashy.
Wildlife Photography Assistant — RAG over EXIF + field guides; suggests lens/settings. Niche but distinctive.

parts.title: Depth & Systems
parts.sub: Weeks 11–18 · post-capstone · Huyen + ByteByteGo + AI system design + interview prep

weeks.title: Foundation Model Internals
weeks.goal: Goal: understand why models behave as they do — the theory you skipped in Part I, now that you can contextualize it against systems you've built. Huyen Ch 2.

days.focusSub: temperature, top-k, top-p, greedy; structured outputs
days.hands: Implement temperature + top-p sampling over raw logprobs; show how each reshapes the output distribution.

days.focusSub: CoT, best-of-N, ToT, parallel/sequential sampling, search-against-verifier
days.hands: Implement best-of-N, self-consistency, and a simple Tree-of-Thoughts + verifier search on a SQL/math task; chart accuracy vs token cost.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 2 — The Probabilistic Nature of AI

days.hands: Run the same prompt 20× at temp 0 vs 1; quantify variance. Design a determinism strategy for billpay-grade reliability.

days.focusSub: SFT, RLHF, DPO; reward modeling (ORM/PRM), STaR, Meta-CoT
days.hands: Read a DPO preference dataset; diagram outcome- vs process-reward models (ORM/PRM) and how STaR bootstraps reasoning data. Sketch preference data for a billpay assistant.

days.focusSub: data corpora (Common Crawl/FineWeb), BPE, params, context, MoE
days.hands: Map the pre-training pipeline (corpora → cleaning → BPE → architecture). Build a decision table: per task class, which model size/family + why.

days.focusSub: optional deep path — what's inside the weights

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Optional: probe attention heads / activations on a small model. Pull the relevant module from Cohen's course — don't attempt all 90h; sample interpretability + attention internals.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 2 + ByteByteGo P1/P4 · Cohen (optional)
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-11-fm-internals — sampling + ToT/verifier eval + reward-model notes + determinism writeup.

weeks.title: Evaluation, Deep
weeks.goal: Goal: the measurement foundations behind Week 7. This is what separates engineers who think their system works from those who can prove it. Huyen Ch 3–4.

days.hands: Compute perplexity of a local model on a held-out billpay-docs set; interpret what it tells you.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 3 — AI as a Judge

days.hands: Build an LLM judge with an explicit rubric; validate against human labels (TPR/TNR); measure position/verbosity bias.
Pairwise-compare 3 models on your task; compute an Elo ranking from the results.

days.focusSub: capability, cost/latency; build vs buy; benchmarks

resources.label: Huyen Ch 4 — Evaluation Criteria + Model Selection

days.hands: Build a model-selection scorecard for billpay (capability × cost × latency × control). Critically read 2 public benchmarks for leakage/mismatch.

days.focusSub: eval all components, guidelines, methods + data

resources.label: Huyen Ch 4 — Design Your Evaluation Pipeline

days.hands: Design an end-to-end eval pipeline for your capstone (component-level + system-level); wire it into CI as a release gate.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 3–4 · Maven AI Evals if investing
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-12-eval — perplexity calc + validated judge + Elo + model scorecard + CI eval gate.

weeks.title: AI System Design
weeks.goal: Goal — bridge from "can build" to "can architect." At top product companies the system-design loop is gatekeeping. Practice 4 canonical AI system designs end-to-end (RAG search, AI coding assistant, recsys + LLMs, agentic at scale), then a mock interview. Your JVM / Temporal / inference-opt edges become concrete answers here, not bullet points.

resources.label: Eugene Yan — System Design for ML
Chip Huyen — Designing ML Systems (intro / Ch 1)
Jay Alammar / others — AI system design write-ups

days.hands: Write your own 7-step AI-systems template: (1) requirements → (2) functional & non-functional → (3) data & eval → (4) model & retrieval → (5) serving / scale / cost → (6) safety & failure modes → (7) iteration & metrics. Use it for every design below.

days.focusSub: "Design GitHub Code Search with LLM answers"
days.hands: 2-page design doc: index size (≈100M chunks), freshness SLO, recall target, latency budget (p95 < 800ms), hybrid retrieval, reranker placement, caching, multi-tenant isolation, eval pipeline. Put real numbers.

resources.label: Cursor / Continue / Aider engineering blog posts

days.hands: Design doc: autocomplete vs chat vs agent modes, context windowing strategy, repo indexing, latency (TTFT < 150ms for autocomplete), local vs cloud model split, privacy, telemetry / eval.
Design doc: candidate generation (collaborative + content embeddings), LLM reranker at the head (cost gate), feature store, cold-start, online learning loop, A/B + Elo for ranker upgrades.

days.focusSub: "Customer-support copilot at 1M tickets / day" — your Temporal lane
days.hands: Design doc: tool inventory, HITL escalation, durable orchestration via Temporal, throttled fan-out (reusing W8 math), inference tier sizing (reusing W15), cost-per-ticket target, failure taxonomy + eval, audit/compliance. Explicitly call out the JVM / Temporal / inference-opt edges.

resources.label: Alex Xu — System Design Interview (Vol 2) chapters on ML

days.hands: Record a 45-min mock for one of designs 2–5 (whiteboard or Excalidraw, talk aloud). Score against the rubric: requirements gathering, tradeoffs, depth on bottleneck, eval strategy, your edges. Write a 1-pager: "my interview-ready angles" — JVM-native AI, Temporal orchestration, inference economics.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Eugene Yan / Huyen / Anthropic posts. No single course fits — interview prep itself is in W18.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-12b-sysdesign — 4 design docs + 1 mock recording + differentiator one-pager.

weeks.title: Advanced RAG & Agents
weeks.goal: Goal: the retrieval and agent depth that actually moves production quality — beyond the basic pipeline you built in Week 3. Huyen Ch 6.

days.hands: Implement BM25 + dense + RRF fusion on your W3 corpus; compare recall across the three.
Add a cross-encoder reranker + query rewriting; measure nDCG lift vs the W3 baseline.
Add tabular retrieval over your Oracle schema — text-to-SQL plus row retrieval — feeding the agent.
Add a memory layer to your capstone agent (vector recall + rolling summary); test multi-session recall.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 6 — Agent Failure Modes and Evaluation

days.hands: Build a failure taxonomy + targeted evals for your agent; add loop/cost circuit-breakers.
Implement Reflexion (self-critique memory) and ReWOO (plan-then-execute) variants of your agent; compare against plain ReAct on your eval set.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 6
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-13-advanced-rag — hybrid+rerank + tabular RAG + agent memory + failure evals.

weeks.title: Finetuning Depth + Dataset Engineering
weeks.goal: Goal: the quantitative grounding behind fine-tuning, and the data work that actually decides whether it works — the half of fine-tuning nobody shows you. Huyen Ch 7–8.

days.hands: Compute VRAM for full vs LoRA vs QLoRA on 7B/13B; verify against your actual Week-6 run.
Quantize your W6 model to Q4/Q5; benchmark quality + tokens/sec at each level.
Merge two LoRA adapters with mergekit; eval the merged model on both source tasks.
Build a 200-example billpay instruction dataset with an explicit quality rubric + coverage check.
Synthesize + distill from a frontier model into your small model; dedup & filter; try RAFT (train the model to ignore distractor docs) and re-run the W6 eval to show lift.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 7–8
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-14-data — memory-math sheet + quantized models + merged adapter + synthetic dataset.

weeks.title: Inference Optimization
weeks.goal: Goal — YOUR SECOND EDGE. Distributed-systems thinking applied to LLM serving: throughput, batching, caching, latency-vs-cost. This is the strongest specialised material in the plan — most AI engineers can't reason about it; you do this for a living. Huyen Ch 9.

days.focusSub: TTFT, TPOT, throughput, goodput; GPU basics
days.hands: Benchmark TTFT / TPOT / throughput of your W6 model under load; build a latency-vs-cost table.
Enable speculative decoding in vLLM; measure the speedup vs baseline.

days.focusSub: continuous batching, KV cache, prefill/decode disaggregation
days.hands: Load-test vLLM continuous batching; chart throughput vs concurrency; observe KV-cache + prefill effects.

days.focusSub: prompt cache, semantic cache, model gateway/router
days.hands: Add prompt + semantic caching; route easy queries to a small model via a gateway; measure $ saved.
Design the serving tier for 500K throttled workflows: concurrency caps, batching, cache-hit targets — without scaling the r8g.8xlarge. This is your interview centerpiece.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 9 — no course matches your background here.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-15-inference — load-test harness + spec-decoding bench + batching chart + gateway/cache + scale design doc.

weeks.title: AI Eng Architecture & Production Deployment
weeks.goal: Goal: assemble everything into a production reference architecture and deploy it to the cloud — the platform-engineering layer that is 70–80% of real production AI work. Huyen Ch 10 + Ed Donner Production Track.

days.focusSub: context → guardrails → router → cache → agents → orchestration
days.hands: Draw your capstone as the full layered architecture; label each layer in your actual code.
Add input + output guardrails to the capstone; red-team with 10 injections; document each defense.

days.focusSub: Docker, AWS Bedrock, SageMaker, Lambda, App Runner, API Gateway
days.hands: Containerize the capstone (Docker); deploy behind API Gateway + Lambda (or App Runner for the long-running agent); call Bedrock as a model provider.
Terraform your infra; GitHub Actions pipeline that runs the Week-12 eval gate before any deploy. Note the Vercel/GCP/Azure equivalents the Production Track covers.
Deploy the multi-agent capstone serverless (Aurora Serverless + SQS); wire Langfuse; add a conversational feedback-capture loop.

Add a thin SaaS layer (auth, usage metering, subscription tier) in front of the capstone; document the MLOps loop — monitoring, retraining triggers, rollback. Optional but makes the project look commercial.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Production Track + Huyen Ch 10
weeks.deliverable: Capstone deployed to AWS, Terraform-managed, eval-gated CI/CD, Langfuse-instrumented, with a feedback loop.

weeks.title: Multimodal & Generative Media
weeks.goal: Optional — breadth, and personally relevant. The least central to a backend-agentic path, but it's an entire domain (and ties to your photography). Spine: ByteByteGo P5. Skip without guilt if you're optimizing purely for agentic-backend roles.

days.hands: One-pager comparing the four families; where diffusion won and why.

days.focusSub: forward/backward process, U-Net vs DiT, sampling
days.hands: Run the HF diffusion-from-scratch notebook; sample at varying step counts and observe quality/latency.

days.focusSub: Stable Diffusion / diffusers; FID, CLIP score
days.hands: Generate images with SDXL via diffusers; evaluate a set with CLIP score; try ControlNet for composition control.

days.focusSub: latent diffusion, DiT for video, T2V systems
days.hands: Read the T2V system design; run an open T2V model on a short clip; note the scaling challenges.
Build a small T2I agent that generates concept references / backdrops from a prompt — a useful pre-shoot tool for your portrait work.

weeks.udemy: Spine: ByteByteGo P5 + HF Diffusion Course
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-17-multimodal — diffusion notebook + T2I generation agent.

weeks.title: Interview Prep, Portfolio & Job Search
weeks.goal: Goal — convert what you built into offers. Everything before this week made you a strong AI engineer; this week makes that legible to a hiring loop. STAR stories from the capstone + Temporal + inference work, take-home rehearsal, GitHub polish, blog posts as proof, target-company outreach. Hireable people skip this and leave money on the table.

days.focusSub: 5 stories that cover the loop — your edges first
days.hands: Write 5 STAR stories (Situation / Task / Action / Result, ≤300 words each): (1) Temporal fan-out at 500K from W8, (2) RAG quality lift from W3/W13, (3) eval-gated CI from W7/W7B, (4) JVM-native AI from W2B, (5) inference cost/latency win from W15. Each must end with a metric.

Find 2 public AI-engineer take-homes ("build a small RAG on this corpus," "design + ship an agent for X"). Do one on a 4-hour timer; ship a working repo with README, evals, and a 1-page writeup. Reflect: where did you go over time, what would you cut next time.

days.focusSub: Recruiters open the repo, not the resume
days.hands: Upgrade the capstone README: architecture diagram (Excalidraw), 60-sec demo gif, eval scoreboard, deploy notes, "what I'd build next." Pin top 5 repos on your profile. Add a one-screen profile README that links the 3 things you want them to read first.

days.focusSub: The W8 + W15 posts you've been drafting

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Why you should write

days.hands: Publish the W8 post ("What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration"). Start the W15 post ("Inference Economics for Agentic Workloads"). Cross-post to your site, dev.to, LinkedIn. These are linkable proof in every conversation.

resources.label: Gergely Orosz — How to find AI engineering jobs

days.hands: Build a target list of 20 companies in 3 buckets — frontier labs / AI-first product / AI-augmented incumbents. Tailor the resume per bucket. Identify 5 warm intros (LinkedIn 2nd-degree) and send messages today. Track in a simple sheet.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none — this week is about your own artifacts, not consuming content.
weeks.deliverable: 5 STAR stories + 1 completed take-home + polished capstone README + 1 published blog post + 20-company target list with 5 outreaches sent.

timeSummary.deliverable: MLflow + Langfuse prompts + HF Hub + DVC + CI eval gate
Capstone on AWS, IaC, SaaS, eval-gated

paidCourses: AI Evals for Engineers & PMs (Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar) — highest-signal paid course in the space (~$2k). Pairs with Weeks 7 & 12.
Ed Donner — AI Engineer Core Track (~$15–20 on sale) — backbone for Weeks 2, 3, 6.
AI Engineer Agentic Track (~$15–20) — 8 projects across OpenAI SDK / CrewAI / LangGraph / AutoGen / MCP; drives Weeks 4–5.
AI Engineer Production Track (~$15–20) — AWS Bedrock/SageMaker, Lambda, Terraform, CI/CD, serverless multi-agent; drives Week 16.
notes.buyingTip: Buying tip: all three Ed Donner courses are on Udemy — the Personal Plan unlocks everything and beats buying individually; otherwise wait for the recurring ~$10–20 sale and never pay sticker. Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (the book you have) is the depth-weeks spine — no course replaces it.
pitfalls: You now own overlapping courses — don't binge them linearly. Use the resource map: one primary per week, the rest as reference. Watching all six Ed Donner courses + ByteByteGo + Cohen end-to-end is hundreds of hours and mostly redundant.
Don't let the depth weeks block shipping. The capstone (W10) is the hireable artifact; W11–18 deepen you but a portfolio project beats half-read theory. Ship first.
Don't chase "the correct framework." Week 5 is breadth on purpose — learn all four, commit to none.

Your backend rigor is a superpower past Week 4. Most AI engineers can't reason about throughput, idempotency, retries, or transactional semantics — your capstone and Week 15 should be 10× more production-ready than the median learner's.
Don't fine-tune unless evals say to. The #1 trap for technical learners.

Your edges are Weeks 2B, 8, and 15. JVM-native AI + Temporal orchestration + inference optimization. Build the capstone on the JVM and write the blog posts — they're your differentiated interview assets.
